title: Stanford CS25: Transformers United V6 I From Language Models to Native Multimodal Intelligence
transcript: All right. So, hello guys and welcome back to uh CS 25. Today, I'm a uh very glad to welcome uh Victoria Lin um from Thinking Machines Lab. Um so, she's a technical member of technical staff there where she focuses on native uh multimodal intelligence. And she is passionate about building AI systems that empower humans to tackle complex knowledge-intensive um problems effectively. And previously, she was a research scientist at Meta AI and Salesforce AI Research. And she received her PhD from the University of um Washington. And without further ado, um let's give it up for Victoria. >> Thank you. Thank you for having me having me. Uh so, can people hear me okay? Um yeah, so I'm very glad to be here to uh today and talk about uh native multimodal language models. So, uh a declaration is this talk is going to be exclusively focused on pub- public materials and um the talk represents my own opinion, not of my employer. So, without further ado, let's start it. Um so, I think without much argument, large language models is one of the most exciting breakthrough happened in the past a few years. Um I think nowadays, probably like everyone sitting in this room will use uh language models uh a little bit like uh during your daily life. Uh so, for example, like uh asking questions to ChatGPT, using like Claude code or Codex to help you to code your project. So, these models were built based on next token prediction over symbolic information, and they uh then and they scale to demonstrate amazing emergent capabilities. So, just uh as a quick recap, uh how how does the today's large language model paradigms works? So, like here's architecture you will be familiar with it. This is a transformer cell. So, basically it carries out next token prediction. Predict the probability over distributed over a vocabulary. And we train such a model using a large number of coppers curated over different domains. And as we notice that as the data and model scales, they start to demonstrate amazing capabilities, such as they can acquire large amount of knowledge. They can be post trained to follow instructions. And they are able to carry out chain of thought reasoning. And and most recently we also found that they are actually able to plan to solve complicated tasks. However, I'm also going to argue that just a language modeling alone is not enough for us. Uh so, why is it? It is because both the digital world we deal with every day and also the physical world we we live in, they are actually filled with information coming from different modalities. Uh like images, audio, video. Uh so, if you surf on the internet, like you will you will you will get what I'm talking about. And also like suppose we are if we are building AI systems that's going to interact with us uh real time in the physical world, we have to deal with multiple multimodal information. So, the goal is to build basically AI systems that's not only processing symbolic knowledge, but is also able to uh seamlessly handle these multimodal information sent to us like in terms of visual and auditory information. And actually, if you pay attention to today's state of the art language model landscape, uh you will see like for a lot of like recent models being rolled out, they have already been claiming that these models are native multi-modal and like are able to process um large amount of multi-modal information, which is true. Uh we see like very amazing capabilities from these models in terms of image, video understanding, video object coding, and so on. So, the first of part of the this talk is just going to dive into the building philosophies of such models. So, what do we call what do we define as native multi-modal language models? I myself personally find it's easier to understand this paradigm by thinking with an analogy of language models. So, the state of the art multi-modal language model architecture is really token performing tokenization across board, which means whether the incoming information comes as image modality, video modality, or is a piece of audio, we try to find a way to convert these signals into token information that can be processed by the transformer. And on top of that, we perform a global auto-regressive generation modeling similar to language models. So, exactly what do I mean? So, for text, we can tokenize them similar as what we do for large language models. Usually, we can use byte pair encoding, break down the text into small pieces of tokens. And for images, turns out we can also do similar things. So, when like a whole piece of image coming in, we can perform an operation which called the patchify. So, basically, we can divide it this image into small parts. And parts is usually defined predefined like by um uh standard size such as uh 16 pixels * 16 pixels. So, once we standardize the image units, we can uh perform we can run for example encoders on these image units and get vector representations of them. And then we sequentialize these vectorizations of the image units and then we got the image tokens. For audio, we can perform similar operation. Uh when the waveform comes in, we can perform transforms on top of the waveform and uh we can apply certain operations basically to tokenize the resulting representation. And uh accordingly, you can encode uh your audio tokens as well. And for videos, so this slide is is dense, but like what's really it's saying is for videos, we can also easily sing them as sequence of images. So, if we think of video uh a video segment as a sequence of image, we can perform the similar patchification on each of the video frame and then we concat it uh concatenate uh the patches of all the images in this video sequence and then we can still represent the the video as a sequence of tokens. So, when I say tokens, they are not necessarily discrete tokens. They can be uh if we encode them into a dense vector, we still refer to them as tokens. And basically, we perform this tokenization across a board and then like we can basically getting multimodal sequence, we can train them in the same way as we train a large language model. Um so, for state-of-the-art multimodal language models, uh when we speak of them, usually there are two broad types of them. So, one type of multimodal uh models consider multimodality as input, but only text modality as output. So, as it's shown here, so like we have a multimodal sequence coming in. Uh we condition them, but we only calculate the losses on the text tokens. So, these can form like uh they this can induce amazing understanding abilities. Like we have an image as input or video as input, the model can start answer questions in text. And in fact like lots of uh multimodal language models you are probably familiarized today like Gemini, Quon, and Kimi, like they they operate in this mode. So, which means it's multimodal input and uh uh text-only output. Of course, like I think these companies have have also developed other models which is able to generate uh multimodal information, but um but a lot of the core products uh is doing text-only out output. So, there is another categories uh of models which is called the omnimodels. Uh so, which means they not only take multimodal information as input, but they also do multimodal information as output. In this case like these models is capable of generating not only text, but also uh images and audios. Uh so, something like you might be familiar with like GPT-4o. So, like which is named Omni because it can also generate images. So, um this is an important slice for today because I think this summarize that by taking this tokenization view, how we could transfer a lot of like the architectural and training principles from the large language modeling paradigm to the multimodal paradigm. So, like there are a couple benefits we can get from it. So, first um by designing such architectures, this allows us to build multimodal models which is able to uh allow us to conduct prompting and instruction following. So, we can prompt these models with mixed model prompts and which are and enable them to solve like complicated tasks which requires multi-modality. These models will also acquire planning and reasoning capabilities and now even more amazingly is they can plan and reason with multi-model information. So, another exciting thing is we also observe that some positive learnings from large language modeling including scaling data and model size that can lead to a performance improvement can also be transferred to multi-model. So, which means like for these models we see significant promise that if we further scale up the number of parameters in them and also like we scale up the training database, their performance is going to get better and better and we're going to see more emergent capabilities. So, the only interesting remaining here is and I think unlike text modeling which has very accurate scaling laws. So, basically people is able to derive the power law equation and fit the coefficients of the these equations. I think the exact scaling law study for multi-model is is under explored. But, on a rough trend we are still seeing like this scaling capability. So, I think overfit for example like trying to find the exact fit of multi-model scaling law could be like a very interesting future research topic. The fourth is a lot of like architectural scaling techniques and also like efficient techniques such as mixture of experts and its variations, we can also apply them directly to multi-model language models to achieve like better scaling performance. So, so next I I basically want to talk about like the second category of multimodal models, which are the the Omni models. Like because my past research group has like has been dedicated to developing such models and I want to present a few variations of these. So, when come to Omni models, because they can generate not only text modality, so there are a few key research questions. So, the first one is how do we enable them to generate the non-text modalities? And the second is is there is there possible transfer between understanding and generation of the non-text modalities? So, with these questions in mind, the first model I'm going to talk about is the Chameleon family. So, these models uh adopt a very simple hypothesis. So, basically the the question it ask is can we actually simply tokenize every modality converting them into discrete tokens? This is like a very powerful view. If it is successful, it basically it will allow us to model the multimodal world as discrete as a sequence of discrete tokens so that a lot of properties of language models could potentially transfer. So, this is basically the hypothesis with the Chameleon models. And the key here is how do we discretize images? It turns out it's not that difficult. So, like remember the patchify view we just talked about? So, for Chameleon basically we just apply another step of vectorization of this image patches. So, how do we do that is like given an image we convert it them into patches. And then like basically we we run continuous encoders on top of them to get the embeddings of these patches. And once we get these patches, we don't stop there. Um in uh we follow it uh we follow it up with a learned vector codebook. Then then basically we can take our computed embeddings and try to match them with a codebook. Basically to uh get to the close closest discrete correspondence of each of each of the patches. And by this way basically we can convert an image into a sequence of um discrete representation. And that's how we tokenize image. It which is really uh by like finding um finding uh the the their corresponding index in the in the codebook. So basically we we discretize the images using the VQ-VAE technique. And then like we put the image and text together as an interleave sequence. And then basically we apply cross-entropy language model training objective on this. So the result mini model basically found that it is able to it is able to uh generate interleave text and image in uh in original order. So like uh here like I'm showing like a a sequence of image and text generated by this model. So basically you can see like we can actually generate like mixed model documents from this this model directly. And and with this representation it will also allow us to to perform multi-tasking similar as like how we do with our language models. So basically we can give these models mixed model prompts and get output from it. Uh such as like we can chat with it uh uh do brainstorming uh compare images and so on. So this is a model that purely trying to apply the large language model technique to mixed model modeling. So it is the one of the first model to show that like if we train from scratch, interleave the text and image sequences, this it can induce multimodal capabilities while preserving like the strong text-only performance. Uh however, we also realize some limitations when we discretize the image. So basically one is like for image understanding tasks, discretization often cause like significant information loss. So as a result, when we compare it to the state-of-the-art multimodal language models nowadays, which is running like a continuous image encoding such as SigLIP, uh there's usually like a significant performance gap in terms of image understanding. And the second is even on the generation side, uh we also found that discrete basically basically discretizing the images and generate them like actually poses some challenges in terms of like token efficiency. So these these models like which means these models typically needs to be trained on large amounts of data to be able to sample like well-formed images. So uh in summary, Chameleon pre-presented a very interesting paradigm where we can uh train text and images interleaved from scratch. Um but we also found limitations. Um basically the assumption that we can discretize uh information in the images might be too strong. So um Transfusion is another uh approach which is pre-proposed that in roughly the similar time as Chameleon. So which basically tries to actually overcome some of the limitations of Chameleon. So Transfusion is a very interesting and also impactful architecture. So basically it it adopts the it it tries to adopt continuous representation of the images uh using them as input, but also tries to directly predict these continuous representation of images. So, how do we predict continuous representation of images? So, if you are familiar with diffusion models, so basically diffusion models is a very successful approach for image generation. And it can that image generation by sampling starting from noise and continuously predict the noise to be removed from this image noise until you arrived at a clear picture. So, basically what transfusion does is it combines auto regressive language modeling and diffusion based image generation in a single transformer. So, the input to a transfusion model is still interleaved text and image sequence. And for the text, we perform standard auto regressive modeling. But for the image part, uh we instead of like doing auto regressive prediction, we take a segment of image representation and basically try to do diffusion operation on it. Uh we can the model can perform multi multiple step of diffusion, get the clear image and then take the clear image as input and and move on auto regressively. So, some some important architectural changes related is like for text, we can continue to use this causal attention structure. And for the images, uh for the sake of better performance, we apply bidirectional uh attention on top of these images. So, this is the transfusion architecture. So, um as as shown in the basically as shown in the transfusion paper, um so, these models is able to quickly generate much better image quality compared to discrete discrete uh discrete token based image generation using much less token budget. So in summary so transfusion is a first approach which seamlessly unify auto regressive next token prediction and the diffusion objective for multimodal modeling. It it demonstrates significantly better image generation quality and token efficiency. On the other hand there is also open research problem because for the trans- transfusion paper it is still trying to represent it is still trying to represent multimodal information in a text and image interleaved sequences. However um it it it it adopts the V representation for the images which is very efficient for generation generating the images but on the other hand these are not very efficient representation for image understanding tasks. So like this creates a dilemma. So which which is still an open problem I would say like in the research field today. Basically the field found that like for image generation and understanding like we sometimes needs two types of encodings for them. So so like state of the art omni models today like often tries to overcome this shortcoming by apply by adopting like two types of image encodings. Cool. So we talked about like two types of omni models like using different representation for the image modality. So what else can we further innovate on this architecture to basically to make them more efficient? So next I'm going to talk about a technique which is which modifies the transformer backbone architecture. So, basically the modif- the motivation comes from that now we have are having the transformers process multimodal information. However, different modalities have like different information density. Um the nature of the data is of also very different when it comes to text and uh and images, for example. So, the question is is it necessary that we have to use a unified transformer parameters to process these um the these modalities, or can we actually introduce something specialized to each modality? So, this basically takes us to um the mix uh so, an architecture which we called mixture of transformers. So, basically the idea behind the mixture of transformers is we try to come up with a independent set of transformer parameters for each of the modalities. So, by independent set of parameters, I'm referring to um such as like QKV projection matrix matrices in the attention layer, as well as like the feed-forward layer. So, basically we have we have a independent set of parameters for transformer parameters for each of the modality. For the text modality, uh as as shown here, like given an input sequence with interleaved modalities, we basically select uh to activate the set of the set of parameters uh based on the modality deterministically. If it is it is a text token, we activate the text set of parameters. If it is if it is an image token, we activate the image parameters. If it's the audio token, we we uh activate the speech parameters. Uh so, so, as shown here, like we we perform this in the attention layer. Once we get the QKV projections separately, we perform a joint attention over these modality tokens to to allow them to unify. Once we get the unified output from the attention layer, we pass them through the feed forward layer. And the feed forward layer here again is divided by modality. So like each modality will have its own feed forward parameters. And once we pass these tokens through the feed forward layer, we got the normal transform output. So basically like from the outside, we still have like transformer unit. We got the token input and output. But like on the inside, the process the processing for each modality happens differently using different set of parameters. And this technique can basically can be combined by with the two models I just talked about. Like with the chameleon models, which is using auto regressive uh cross entropy objective for each of the modality tokens. Or like with the transfusion technique, which is using auto regressive objective for text and diffusion objective for images.
